teachingPoint: When publishing a shared versioned resource, two concurrent publishers can both read the same version pin and overwrite each other silently. storeDocumentWithOCC(data, expectedVersionPin) protects against this: the second writer receives OptimisticConcurrencyConflict. On conflict: emit SchemaPublishConflict — never retry silently.
positiveExample: getDocumentWithVersion() to read versionPin, then storeDocumentWithOCC(schema, versionPin); on OCC_CONFLICT emit SchemaPublishConflict
negativeExample: storeDocument(schema) — plain write with no version pin, last-write-wins silence
discriminatingConstraint: storeDocumentWithOCC() not plain storeDocument() — OCC mandatory for shared versioned resources
keywords: occ optimistic concurrency control schema publish version pin storeDocumentWithOCC getDocumentWithVersion

teachingPoint: Graph cycle detection requires tracking nodes on the active DFS traversal path (GRAY state), not just completed nodes (BLACK). A single visited Set conflates these two states. Back edges — the signature of a cycle — are only visible when a GRAY node is encountered again during traversal. Mark nodes GRAY on entry and BLACK on exit. A GRAY encounter during DFS is a cycle; a BLACK encounter is a safe cross-edge.
positiveExample: DFS marks nodes WHITE→GRAY on entry, GRAY→BLACK on exit; GRAY node encounter = CYCLE_DETECTED with cyclePath populated
negativeExample: visited = new Set(); if (visited.has(node)) return; — single-color, cannot detect back edges, A→B→A passes as valid
discriminatingConstraint: DFS uses GRAY marking for back-edge detection — not a single visited Set
keywords: dag cycle detection dfs transitive closure graph white gray black back edge visited two-color

teachingPoint: Schema changes must be classified as ADDITIVE (new optional field, new enum value) or BREAKING (removed field, type change, renamed required field) before any write. ADDITIVE changes auto-proceed to publish. BREAKING changes must gate on an approval workflow. Publishing a BREAKING change without approval is a SILENT_FAILURE: no error is thrown, but all documents validated against the prior schema are immediately corrupted.
positiveExample: T190 classifies changeType at entry gate; BREAKING routes to SchemaApprovalRequired; ADDITIVE proceeds to DAG cycle check then publish
negativeExample: all schema versions auto-publish regardless of changeType — BREAKING immediately becomes current version without approval
discriminatingConstraint: BREAKING changeType routes to approval workflow — never directly to publish
keywords: breaking additive schema change classification approval gate version evolution ADDITIVE BREAKING

teachingPoint: When a state-changing event triggers an expensive read-heavy rebuild, the rebuild must be time-decoupled from the synchronous response. The submitter needs confirmation that their schema is registered — not that the global topology has been updated. Blocking the HTTP response on DAG rebuild couples a fast write to a slow aggregation. Trigger via queue event, complete independently.
positiveExample: T192 DagDependencyTracker is @EventPattern('SchemaPublished') consumer; HTTP response returns before rebuild begins; DagRebuildCompleted fires asynchronously
negativeExample: T189 calls T192.rebuild() inline and awaits before returning HTTP response — DAG rebuild latency blocks all schema submissions
discriminatingConstraint: DAG rebuild triggered by queue event after publish — never inline before HTTP response
keywords: dag rebuild async decoupled topology schema publish time decoupled queue event

teachingPoint: A document that was valid at creation time must remain validatable against the schema version it was generated against. Pin the schemaVersion field on every generated document at creation time. Validate against the pinned version — never against the current (latest) version. Validating against current silently invalidates historically correct documents whenever a BREAKING schema change is published.
positiveExample: document stores schemaVersion: 'v1.0' at creation; T205 validates against schemaVersion from the document, not against getCurrentVersion()
negativeExample: T205 validates against getCurrentVersion(schemaType) — document that was correct at v1.0 fails against v2.0 BREAKING change
discriminatingConstraint: validate against document's pinned schemaVersion — never against current schema version
keywords: schema version pin validation drift generated document schemaVersion field pinned
optimistic concurrency control OCC storeDocumentWithOCC getDocumentWithVersion version pin concurrent publishers SchemaPublishConflict
teachingPoint: Shared versioned resources (schemas, configs, plans) that multiple writers may update concurrently MUST use OCC. Plain storeDocument() is last-write-wins — two concurrent publishers overwrite each other silently. OCC pattern: read with version pin, write with expected version, surface conflict explicitly as an event rather than a silent overwrite.
ironRules: Read with getDocumentWithVersion() to capture seqNo+primaryTerm version pin
Write with storeDocumentWithOCC(data, versionPin) — not plain storeDocument()
On OCC_CONFLICT: emit SchemaPublishConflict — NEVER retry silently
Any plain storeDocument() on a shared versioned resource = BUILD_FAILURE

keywords: three-state DFS WHITE GRAY BLACK back edge cycle detection DAG visited Set diamond pattern false positive
teachingPoint: Graph cycle detection requires three color states — not a single visited Set. A single Set conflates 'currently being traversed' (GRAY) with 'already completed' (BLACK). Back edges (A→B→A) are only detectable by re-encountering a GRAY node. A single Set produces false negatives on diamond DAGs (A→B→D and A→C→D both visit D, second visit incorrectly flags cross-edge as cycle).
ironRules: DFS marks nodes WHITE on init, GRAY on entry, BLACK on exit
GRAY node encountered during traversal = cycle confirmed (back edge)
BLACK node encountered = safe cross-edge or forward edge — NOT a cycle
Single visited Set cannot distinguish GRAY from BLACK = BUILD_FAILURE for diamond DAGs

keywords: breaking additive schema change classification approval workflow auto-proceed gate BREAKING ADDITIVE
teachingPoint: Schema changes must be classified at entry before any write. ADDITIVE changes (new optional fields, new enum values) auto-proceed — they cannot break existing documents. BREAKING changes (removed fields, type changes, renamed required fields) require approval workflow — they silently corrupt existing documents if published without approval.
ironRules: Classification runs at ORDER 1 in T190 before any storeDocument or enqueue
BREAKING → route to SchemaApprovalRequired — never directly to publish
ADDITIVE → proceed to DAG cycle check then publish
All schema changes classified — unclassified = BUILD_FAILURE

keywords: async time-decoupled DAG rebuild queue event fast write slow aggregation topology submit confirm
teachingPoint: When a fast write triggers a slow downstream computation (DAG rebuild, topology update, index recompute), the computation MUST be time-decoupled via queue event. The HTTP response confirms the write — not the downstream computation. Inline await of the computation couples fast write latency to slow aggregation latency, destroying throughput under concurrent load.
ironRules: T192 DagDependencyTracker is @EventPattern('SchemaPublished') consumer — never called inline
HTTP response (or DataProcessResult.success) returns BEFORE DAG rebuild begins
DagRebuildCompleted fires asynchronously — consumers must not depend on synchronous completion
Any inline await of rebuild before response = BUILD_FAILURE
